title: Wrapper Functions
text: can handle models including random effects (varying effects) using (1 | group) notation, similar lme4 package. Let’s build model taking account individual sat varies depending group belong .
code: # Random intercept model mdl_glmer <- rtmb_glmer(sat ~ talk + (1 | group),   data = debate )  # When including random effects, optimize() with Laplace approximation is fast opt_glmer <- mdl_glmer$optimize(laplace = TRUE) opt_glmer$summary() ## Call: ## MAP Estimation via RTMB ##  ## Negative Log-Posterior: 404.65 ## Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -412.59 ## Note: Random effects are stored in $random_effects ##  ## Point Estimates and 95% Wald CI: ##    variable  Estimate  Std. Error  Lower 95%  Upper 95%  ## Intercept     2.60450     0.17902    2.25363    2.95537  ## b[talk]       0.27441     0.05477    0.16706    0.38176  ## sigma         0.77389     0.03867    0.70170    0.85351  ## sd[Int]       0.51833     0.06587    0.40406    0.66492  ## Intercept_c   3.43322     0.06846    3.29904    3.56740

title: Wrapper Functions
text: rtmb_lm, rtmb_glm, rtmb_glmer, can use regularization. Regularization method shrinks coefficients excessive parameters towards 0, allowing construction parsimonious models high predictive power. BayesRTMB package, can select horseshoe prior (rhs) spike--slab prior (ssp) using penalty option. rhs relatively easier estimate, coefficients small effects shrink exactly 0. ssp somewhat heavier estimate, can shrink coefficients completely 0. using regularization, must also use weakly informative priors, means need input range objective variable. Since data uses 5-point scale, input c(1, 5). MAP estimation often struggles regularized models, ’s better use MCMC.
code: mdl_glmer <-   rtmb_glmer(     sat ~ talk + perf + skill + cond + (1 | group),     data = debate,     penalty = "ssp",     y_range = c(1, 5)   )  mcmc_glmer <- mdl_glmer$sample(parallel = TRUE) mcmc_glmer mcmc_glmer$draws("b[cond]") |> plot_dens()

title: Wrapper Functions

title: Wrapper Functions
text: conditional_effects() function used visualize predicted values model. particularly powerful understanding interaction effects.
code: # Linear regression with interaction between talk and cond mdl_int <- rtmb_lm(sat ~ talk * perf, data = debate) fit_int <- mdl_int$sample()  # Visualize the interaction effect # For continuous moderators, it automatically shows Mean +/- 1SD ce <- conditional_effects(fit_int, effect = "talk:perf") plot(ce)

title: Wrapper Functions
text: conditional_effects() provides visual overview, simple_effects() allows statistically examine effect focal variable specific levels moderator. Simple Slopes: focal variable continuous. Pairwise Contrasts: focal variable categorical. default, continuous moderators, evaluates effect Mean Mean +/- 1SD. can change behavior specifying sd_multiplier argument.
code: # Calculate simple slopes of 'talk' for each level of 'cond' se <- simple_effects(fit_int, effect = "talk:perf") print(se) ## --- Simple Effects Analysis --- ##    moderator perf          term estimate  lower upper ##  perf       2.930 Slope of talk    0.038 -0.118 0.191 ##  perf       4.690 Slope of talk    0.266  0.161 0.369 ##  perf       6.450 Slope of talk    0.494  0.354 0.631

Since regularized factor analysis can sometimes difficult estimate via MAP, usually better use MCMC ADVI. , present results ADVI (Automatic Differentiation Variational Inference). output summary MCMC ADVI allows access via EAP MAP. regularization, MAP summary makes output easier read shrunk parameters rigorously collapse 0.

title: Wrapper Functions
text: rtmb_irt() constructs Item Response Theory (IRT) models suitable analyzing test data questionnaire surveys. Currently, following models supported: 1PL / 2PL / 3PL Models: Analysis binary data (correct/incorrect). Graded Response Model (GRM): Analysis polytomous data (e.g., Likert scales). IRT models computationally demanding, highly effective utilize fast approximation via variational() marginalize ability parameters using optimize(laplace = TRUE) handling large datasets.

title: Wrapper Functions
text: Using BigFive personality data (polytomous), fit Graded Response Model (GRM). , analyze four items related “Agreeableness”.
code: data(BigFive) # Select items 2, 7, 12, 17 (Agreeableness) dat_irt <- BigFive[, c("BF2", "BF7", "BF12", "BF17")]  # Fit a 2PL Graded Response Model (Ordered Logistic) mdl_irt <- rtmb_irt(dat_irt, model = "2PL", type = "ordered")  # Fast estimation using MAP with Laplace approximation for latent traits opt_irt <- mdl_irt$optimize(se_sampling = TRUE) opt_irt ## Call: ## MAP Estimation via RTMB ##  ## Negative Log-Posterior: 855.97 ## Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -862.01 ## Note: Random effects are stored in $random_effects ##  ## Point Estimates and 95% Wald CI: ##           variable  Estimate  Std. Error  Lower 95%  Upper 95%  ## a[BF2]               2.59656     0.39595    1.90122    3.46560  ## a[BF7]               2.54272     0.35182    1.95367    3.32212  ## a[BF12]              2.53327     0.39717    1.93398    3.53325  ## a[BF17]              1.92822     0.27020    1.46972    2.50115  ## b[BF2,Threshold1]   -5.52251     0.72815   -6.91884   -4.06858  ## b[BF7,Threshold1]   -5.34943     0.64569   -6.63549   -4.19742  ## b[BF12,Threshold1]  -5.29023     0.65049   -6.54949   -4.01624  ## b[BF17,Threshold1]  -4.31129     0.50114   -5.32272   -3.36251  ## b[BF2,Threshold2]   -2.68286     0.42440   -3.41160   -1.76330  ## b[BF7,Threshold2]   -2.72371     0.39514   -3.49773   -1.93475

title: Wrapper Functions
text: BayesRTMB wrapper functions feature standardized missing argument handle missing values (NA) gracefully. can choose appropriate method depending analysis type. default wrappers "listwise", advanced multivariate wrappers support Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML) Pairwise methods.

title: Wrapper Functions
text: data contains missing responses, can easily enable FIML keep respondents model estimate factor loadings using available data:
code: data(BigFive) # Introduce some random missingness set.seed(42) BigFive_miss <- BigFive for (col in 1:ncol(BigFive_miss)) {   BigFive_miss[sample(1:nrow(BigFive_miss), 10), col] <- NA }  # Run Factor Analysis with FIML mdl_fa <- rtmb_fa(BigFive_miss, nfactors = 5, missing = "fiml") fit_fa <- mdl_fa$optimize()

title: Wrapper Functions
text: want match R’s default correlation tests using pairwise deletion, can specify missing = "pairwise":
code: # Classic Pearson correlation with pairwise deletion mdl_corr <- rtmb_corr(BigFive_miss[, 1:5], method = "pearson", missing = "pairwise") fit_corr <- mdl_corr$classic() fit_corr$summary()

title: Wrapper Functions
text: leveraging wrapper functions BayesRTMB, gain following benefits: - Perform sophisticated analyses using familiar syntax without writing complex model codes scratch. - Seamlessly switch MAP, MCMC, VB estimations using model object. - Calculate Bayes factors smoothly. detailed guide mixed models GLMMs, see Hierarchical Models GLMMs. require customized model, check Writing Model Codes try building using rtmb_code(). model-building pipeline behind wrappers, see RTMB Internals Inference Algorithms. compact reference fit-object methods, model comparison, fixed parameters, distributions, see Analysis Reference.

what: 1. Overview of the Structure and Role of rtmb_code
title: Writing Model Codes
text: rtmb_code() function allows define Bayesian models using intuitive syntax similar Stan. model described dividing several “blocks” according purposes.
code: library(BayesRTMB)  code <- rtmb_code(   setup = {     # Data preprocessing and constant definitions   },   parameters = {     # Declaration of parameters to be estimated   },   transform = {     # Calculation of derived variables using parameters and data   },   model = {     # Definition of priors and likelihoods   },   generate = {     # Calculation of posterior predictions and generated quantities   } )

Parameter Types (Dim)” details).
code: parameters = {   # Scalar parameter (with lower bound of 0)   sigma = Dim(lower = 0)      # Vector parameter (length N)   mu = Dim(N)      # Matrix parameter (N rows, M columns)   X = Dim(c(N, M))    # Array parameter (I x J x K)   A = Dim(c(I, J, K)) }

title: Writing Model Codes
text: heart model define priors likelihood (data generating process). Similar Stan, using ~ (tilde) sampling syntax recommended. model can also written explicitly adding log densities: words, Y ~ normal(mu, sigma) lp <- lp + normal_lpdf(Y, mu, sigma) two ways write model. ~ syntax usually easier read, lp <- lp + ..._lpdf(...) useful need build custom likelihood. lp reserved accumulating log probability, declare parameters block use ordinary working variable.
code: model = {   # Priors   mu ~ normal(0, 10)   sigma ~ exponential(1)    # Likelihood   Y ~ normal(mu, sigma) } model = {   lp <- lp + normal_lpdf(mu, 0, 10)   lp <- lp + exponential_lpdf(sigma, 1)   lp <- lp + normal_lpdf(Y, mu, sigma) }

title: Writing Model Codes
text: can also define custom log-density function setup block use sampling syntax. function name end _lpdf continuous distributions _lpmf discrete distributions. example, exgaussian_lpdf() can used model block Y ~ exgaussian(mu, sigma, lambda). following example simulates response times seconds ex-Gaussian distribution fits model. , lambda rate exponential component. user-defined distribution placed setup. model block can use sampling syntax built-distributions. model, helpful provide initial values scale observed response times. model can sampled MCMC fit variational inference. WAIC needed, generate block save observation-wise log_lik calling custom _lpdf sum = FALSE.
code: library(BayesRTMB)  simulate_exgaussian <- function(N, mu, sigma, lambda) {   rnorm(N, mean = mu, sd = sigma) + rexp(N, rate = lambda) }  set.seed(1234)  true <- c(mu = 0.45, sigma = 0.06, lambda = 8) rt <- simulate_exgaussian(   N = 500,   mu = true["mu"],   sigma = true["sigma"],   lambda = true["lambda"] )  hist(rt) code_exgaussian <- rtmb_code(   setup = {     Y <- rt      exgaussian_lpdf <- function(x, mu, sigma, lambda, sum = TRUE) {       z <- (x - mu) / sigma - lambda * sigma        res <-         log(lambda) +         lambda * (mu - x) +         0.5 * (lambda * sigma)^2 +         RTMB::pnorm(z, log.p = TRUE)        if (sum) sum(res) else res     }   },    parameters = {     mu     <- Dim(lower = 0)     sigma  <- Dim(lower = 0)     lambda <- Dim(lower = 0)   },    model = {     Y ~ exgaussian(mu, sigma, lambda)      mu     ~ exponential(1)     sigma  ~ exponential(1)     lambda ~ exponential(1 / 10)   },    generate = {     log_lik <- exgaussian_lpdf(Y, mu, sigma, lambda, sum = FALSE)     report(log_lik)   } ) init_exgaussian <- list(   mu = median(rt) - sd(rt) / 2,   sigma = sd(rt) / 2,   lambda = 1 / (sd(rt) / 2) )  mdl_exgaussian <- rtmb_model(   data = list(rt = rt),   code = code_exgaussian,   init = init_exgaussian )  fit_map <- mdl_exgaussian$optimize(num_estimate = 5)  est_map <- unlist(fit_map$estimate(pars = "parameters"))[names(true)] data.frame(   parameter = names(true),   true = as.numeric(true),   estimate = as.numeric(est_map),   error = as.numeric(est_map - true) ) fit_mcmc <- mdl_exgaussian$sample(parallel = TRUE) fit_mcmc fit_mcmc$WAIC()  fit_vb <- mdl_exgaussian$variational() fit_vb

title: Writing Model Codes
text: parameters block, define types dimensions parameters using Dim(). Scalar: Dim() Dim(1) Vector: Dim(N) Matrix: Dim(c(N, M)) Array: Dim(c(, J, K)) can also enforce various constraints using arguments: * lower = 0, upper = 1: Specifies range values (e.g., variances probabilities). * random = TRUE: Designates parameter random effect subject Laplace approximation hierarchical models. Furthermore, specifying specific keyword type argument, can define complex types specific structures.

title: Writing Model Codes
text: Numerous numerically stable utility functions available prevent overflow underflow Automatic Differentiation (AD) calculations. Link Inverse Link Functions Functions Numerical Stabilization Matrix Vector Transformations useful transforming unconstrained vector matrix specific structure. Linear Algebra AD

title: Writing Model Codes
text: models, fastest simplest approach write vectorized code. example, prefer single vectorized likelihood Y ~ normal(mu, sigma) manually filling temporary vector inside loop. need mutable working vector array whose elements later assigned AD values, use rtmb_vector() rtmb_array() instead numeric(), matrix(), array(). Inside rtmb_code(), BayesRTMB usually provides AD seed automatically model parameters, normally need set seed argument. helpers mainly assignment-heavy code. calculation can expressed directly vectorized matrix array operations, vectorized form usually preferable tape construction repeated AD evaluation.
code: model = {   eta <- rtmb_vector(0, N)   for (i in seq_len(N)) {     eta[i] <- alpha + X[i, ] %*% beta   }   Y ~ normal(eta, sigma) } model = {   logit_x <- rtmb_array(0, dim = c(N_time, C, D))   for (t in seq_len(N_time)) {     for (c in seq_len(C)) {       for (d in seq_len(D)) {         logit_x[t, c, d] <- alpha[d] + beta[d] * X[t, c]       }     }   } }

text: Language / 言語: English introduction | パッケージ紹介 BayesRTMB R package writing fitting statistical models RTMB automatic differentiation engine. can start wrapper functions rtmb_lm(), rtmb_glmer(), rtmb_corr(), rtmb_ttest(), write model rtmb_code(). model object can used MCMC, MAP estimation, variational inference, frequency-oriented classical analyses supported.

title: Bayesian Inference Using RTMB
text: Model code R: Write models setup, parameters, transform, model, generate blocks. Multiple estimation methods: Use $sample(), $optimize(), $variational(), $classic() common model object. Wrapper functions: Fit regression models, GLM/GLMMs, t tests, correlations, contingency tables, factor analysis, IRT, latent rank models, multidimensional unfolding models. Classical analyses wrappers: Use $classic() frequency-oriented outputs, including AIC(), BIC(), anova() supported fits. Random effects Laplace approximation: Declare parameters random = TRUE use RTMB’s Laplace machinery latent variables mixed models. Diagnostics visualization: Plot posterior draws, convergence diagnostics, forest plots, conditional effects, related summaries.

title: Bayesian Inference Using RTMB
text: standard analyses, start wrapper function. can also write model directly.
code: library(BayesRTMB) data(debate)  mdl <- rtmb_lm(sat ~ talk * perf, data = debate)  fit_mcmc <- mdl$sample() fit_map  <- mdl$optimize() fit_lm   <- mdl$classic() Y <- debate$sat X <- debate[c("talk","perf")] |> as.matrix()  data_list <- list(Y = Y, X = X)  code <- rtmb_code(   setup = {     N <- length(Y)     K <- ncol(X)   },   parameters = {     Intercept <- Dim(1)     b <- Dim(K)     sigma = Dim(lower = 0)   },   model = {     mu <- Intercept + X %*% b     Y ~ normal(mu, sigma)     Intercept ~ normal(0, 10)     b ~ normal(0, 10)     sigma ~ exponential(1)   } )  mdl_custom <- rtmb_model(data_list, code) fit_custom <- mdl_custom$sample()

title: Bayesian Inference Using RTMB
text: Introduction: overall concepts, entry points, inference workflow. Quick Start: installation, minimal model, MCMC diagnostics, visualization, t tests. Wrapper Functions: using wrappers Bayesian classical analyses. Hierarchical Models GLMMs: detailed use rtmb_glmer() mixed models, GLMMs, priors, residual correlation, visualization. Writing Model Codes: writing custom models rtmb_code(). RTMB Internals Inference Algorithms: internal model representation, Laplace approximation, inference pipelines. Analysis Reference: function, fit-object, model-comparison, distribution, AD-taping reference. 日本語: パッケージ紹介 日本語: クイックスタート 日本語: ラッパー関数の使い方 日本語: モデルコードの書き方 日本語: 分析リファレンス

code: ADVI_method(   model,   par_list,   pl_full,   iter = 3000,   tol_rel_obj = 0.001,   window_size = 100,   num_samples = 1000,   alpha = 0.01,   laplace = FALSE,   print_freq = 500,   method = c("meanfield", "fullrank", "hybrid"),   update_progress = NULL,   update_interval = 100 )

title: Automatic Differentiation Variational Inference (ADVI) — ADVI_method
text: model RTMB objective function object (`ad_obj`). par_list list defining structure parameters estimated. pl_full list defining full structure parameters including random effects. iter Integer; fixed number iterations optimization. Default 3000. tol_rel_obj Numeric; relative tolerance ELBO check convergence. Default 0.001. window_size Integer; size moving window calculate median ELBO. Default 100. num_samples Integer; number posterior draws generate optimization. Default 1000. alpha Numeric; learning rate (step size) Adam optimizer. Default 0.01. laplace Logical; whether Laplace approximation used. Default FALSE. print_freq Integer; frequency printing progress console. Set 0 disable. Default 500. method Character; type variational distribution. One "meanfield", "fullrank", "hybrid". Default "meanfield". update_progress Optional function progress reporting. update_interval Integer; interval progress updates. Default 100.

title: Automatic Differentiation Variational Inference (ADVI) — ADVI_method
text: list containing `fit`, `random_fit`, `elbo_history`, `elbo_final`, `rel_obj_final`, `converged`.

what: BayesRTMB: Bayesian Modeling with RTMB — BayesRTMB-package
title: BayesRTMB: Bayesian Modeling with RTMB — BayesRTMB-package
text: `BayesRTMB` package provides intuitive, R-based Bayesian modeling environment using `RTMB` package computational backend. allows users perform Maximum Posteriori (MAP) estimation, Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, Automatic Differentiation Variational Inference (ADVI) hierarchical complex non-linear models unified user-friendly interface.

title: BayesRTMB: Bayesian Modeling with RTMB — BayesRTMB-package
text: standard workflow package consists three main steps: 1. **Model Definition**: Use [rtmb_code()] define model structure    (likelihood, prior distributions, transformations, etc.) using standard R syntax. 2. **Model Instantiation**: Pass data generated model code    [rtmb_model()] create `RTMB_Model` R6 object. 3. **Estimation**: Call appropriate method model object:    * `$optimize()`: MAP estimation Laplace approximation.    * `$sample()`: MCMC sampling using -U-Turn Sampler (NUTS).    * `$variational()`: approximate Bayesian inference using ADVI. detailed examples tutorials, please refer package vignettes.

title: BayesRTMB: Bayesian Modeling with RTMB — BayesRTMB-package
text: Maintainer: Hiroshi Shimizu simizu706@gmail.com

text: model `RTMB_Model` object used estimation. fit Posterior draws model parameters. random_fit Posterior draws random effects. transform_fit Posterior draws transformed parameters. transform_dims Dimension information transformed parameters. generate_fit Posterior draws generated quantities. generate_dims Dimension information generated quantities. eps Step size used sampler. accept Acceptance statistics sampling. treedepth Tree depth used HMC/NUTS sampling. n_leapfrog Number leapfrog steps retained draws. divergent Logical matrix indicating divergent transitions. energy Hamiltonian energy retained draws. metric Per-chain inverse mass matrix used NUTS. metric_type Effective mass matrix adaptation type used NUTS. metric_requested Mass matrix adaptation type requested user. metric_effective Effective mass matrix adaptation type chain. metric_auto Automatic metric selection details chain, used. metric_init Initial metric source used NUTS. metric_adaptation Metric learning mode used warmup. nuts_variant NUTS proposal selection variant used sampling. warmup_diagnostics Per-chain warmup diagnostics. laplace Logical; whether Laplace approximation used. posterior_mean Posterior mean estimates. log_ml Numeric value storing calculated log marginal likelihood bridge sampling. comparison_fit MCMC_Fit object containing fitted comparison model. max_treedepth Maximum tree depth requested NUTS/HMC. pd_error_count Positive-definite/singularity errors treated lp = -Inf chain.

title: RTMB model object — RTMB_Model-class
text: R6 class representing Bayesian model built RTMB. class stores model components provides methods building automatic differentiation object, optimizing posterior, drawing posterior samples.

title: RTMB model object — RTMB_Model-class
text: data Named list containing observation data. par_list Evaluated list parameter definitions. log_prob Executable function likelihood prior calculations. transform Function calculating transformed parameters. generate Function calculating generated quantities. par_names List variable names corresponding parameter dimensions. pl_full Complete parameter list expanded constraints dimension information used internally. formula Formula used model generation (created via wrapper function). requested_contrasts Character; user-requested contrast setting (e.g. "treatment"). contrasts Character; internal contrast setting used fitting (e.g. "sum"). raw_data Data frame; original data used model. family Character string distribution family (created via wrapper function). init List numeric vector initial values. view Character vector variable names prioritize summary. code Original model AST generated `rtmb_code()`. map list specifying parameter mappings fixing values (e.g., via fixed_model()). prior_correction Numeric value legacy low-level marginal-likelihood correction. Fixed-parameter prior removal handled AD objective construction. fixed_prior_specs Internal specification removing fixed-parameter prior contributions AD evaluation. type Character; type model (e.g. 'lmer', 'glm', 'table', 'ttest', 'corr', 'mediation'). extra List; used store auxiliary data (like term assignments, factors, test results) needed methods classic().

Default 1. control list control settings passed optimizer. optimizer Character; optimizer use: "nlminb" (default) "optim". method Character; method "optim" (e.g., "BFGS", "L-BFGS-B"). map Optional list specifying parameters fix (factors). fixed Optional list specifying parameter values fix. se_method Character; method uncertainty estimation. "wald": Wald standard errors Wald confidence intervals. "sampling": Simulation-based error propagation. "none": compute standard errors, confidence intervals, degrees freedom. num_samples Integer; number samples draw se_method "sampling". Default 1000. seed Integer; random seed sampling. marginal Character vector "auto" "none"; specifies parameters marginalize via Laplace approximation. "none" (default): additional marginalization. "auto": Uses variables specified model wrapper (e.g., fixed effects lmer). "": Marginalize parameters (advanced use). character vector: Specific parameter names marginalize. df_method Character numeric; method finite degrees--freedom approximation. Used `marginal` resolves one parameters `se_method != "none"`. "auto": Uses Satterthwaite approximation marginalization active. "satterthwaite": Satterthwaite approximation. "inf" "none": Use infinite degrees freedom. numeric: Use supplied fixed degrees freedom. view Character vector parameter names prioritize summary display. ... Additional arguments.

code: RTMB_Model$sample(   sampling = 1000,   warmup = 1000,   chains = 4,   thin = 1,   seed = sample.int(1e+06, 1),   delta = 0.8,   max_treedepth = 10,   nuts_variant = c("multinomial", "slice"),   metric = c("auto", "diag", "dense", "hybrid"),   metric_init = c("identity", "hessian"),   metric_adaptation = c("stan_window", "cumulative", "window"),   metric_regularization = TRUE,   metric_shrinkage = 5,   metric_min = 1e-06,   metric_max = 1e+06,   parallel = FALSE,   laplace = FALSE,   init = NULL,   init_jitter = 0.1,   save_csv = NULL,   map = NULL,   fixed = NULL,   globals = FALSE,   progress = c("auto", "none", "bar", "message") )

title: RTMB model object — RTMB_Model-class
text: sampling Number sampling iterations. Default 1000. warmup Number warmup iterations. Default 1000. chains Number MCMC chains. Default 4. thin Thinning interval. Default 1. seed Random seed. delta Target acceptance rate HMC/NUTS. Default 0.8. max_treedepth Maximum tree depth HMC/NUTS. Default 10. nuts_variant NUTS proposal selection variant. `"multinomial"` uses Stan-style Hamiltonian-weighted proposal selection; `"slice"` uses original slice-based implementation. Default `"multinomial"`. metric Mass matrix adaptation type. `"auto"` starts hybrid strategy falls back `"diag"` warmup dense covariance block unstable low correlation; `"diag"` adapts marginal variances; `"dense"` adapts full covariance metric correlated parameters; `"hybrid"` uses dense metric fixed parameters diagonal metric random parameters. Default `"auto"`. metric_init Initial metric source. `"identity"` starts unit metric; `"hessian"` starts inverse Hessian approximation chain initial value available. Default `"identity"`. metric_adaptation Metric learning mode warmup. `"stan_window"` uses current expanding Stan-style slow window; `"cumulative"` uses slow-window warmup draws accumulated far; `"window"` uses current adaptation window. Default `"stan_window"`. metric_regularization Logical; whether shrink adapted metric toward unit metric warmup. Default TRUE. metric_shrinkage Non-negative scalar controlling strength metric shrinkage. Larger values shrink strongly toward 1. Default 5. metric_min Minimum allowed diagonal metric value. Default 1e-6. metric_max Maximum allowed diagonal metric value. Default 1e6. parallel Logical; whether run chains parallel. Default FALSE. laplace Logical; whether use Laplace approximation. Default FALSE. init Optional initial values parameters. init_jitter sd randomize initial values parameters. save_csv Optional list saving MCMC results. e.g., list(name = "model", dir = "BayesRTMB_mcmc"). map Optional list specifying parameters fix (factors). fixed Optional list specifying parameter values fix. globals Logical; TRUE, let future discover globals automatically parallel = TRUE. FALSE, model object components required BayesRTMB workers exported. Default FALSE. progress Progress reporting style. `"auto"`, `"bar"`, `"message"` use line-based progress messages; `"none"` suppresses detailed progress still prints high-level start message unless silent = TRUE used creating model. `"bar"` accepted backward compatibility. Default `"auto"`.

FALSE, model object components required BayesRTMB workers exported. title: Calculate Bayes Factor — bayes_factor
text: object class `bayes_factor` containing Bayes factor, log Bayes factor,   approximate estimation error, interpretation.

title: Calculate Bayes Factor — bayes_factor
code: # \donttest{   # Compare two models using Bayes Factor   data(debate, package = "BayesRTMB")   fit1 <- rtmb_lm(sat ~ talk, data = debate) #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...   fit2 <- rtmb_lm(sat ~ talk + perf, data = debate) #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...   map1 <- fit1$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>    map2 <- fit2$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>    bf <- bayes_factor(map1, map2)   print(bf) #> Bayes Factor (BF12) : 0  #> Log Bayes Factor    : -10.3811 #> Evidence            : Decisive evidence for Model 2  # }

what: Probability Distributions for RTMB Models — distributions
title: Probability Distributions for RTMB Models — distributions
text: page summarizes probability distributions available within `rtmb_code` block. functions designed used Stan-like sampling syntax (`~`), internally adds log-density model's total log-posterior.

title: Probability Distributions for RTMB Models — distributions
text: Syntax Styles: `rtmb_code`, can specify distributions two ways: Sampling Syntax (Recommended): y ~ normal(mu, sigma) Explicit Function Call: lp <- lp + normal_lpdf(y, mu, sigma) Continuous Distributions (LPDF): normal(mean, sd): Normal distribution. lognormal(meanlog, sdlog): Lognormal distribution. exp_mod_normal(mu, sigma, lambda): Exponentially modified normal distribution. exponential(rate): Exponential distribution. cauchy(location, scale): Cauchy distribution. student_t(df, mu, sigma): Student's t-distribution. gamma(shape, rate): Gamma distribution. inverse_gamma(shape, scale): Inverse-gamma distribution. beta(, b): Beta distribution. Discrete Distributions (LPMF): bernoulli(prob) / bernoulli_logit(eta): Binary outcomes. binomial(size, prob) / binomial_logit(size, eta): Binomial outcomes. poisson(mean): Poisson count data. neg_binomial_2(mu, size): Negative binomial (mean/dispersion parameterization). ordered_logistic(eta, cutpoints): Ordered categorical outcomes. sequential_logistic(eta, cutpoints): Sequential ordered categorical outcomes. Multivariate Matrix Distributions: multi_normal(mean, Sigma): Standard multivariate normal distribution. multi_student_t(df, mean, Sigma): Multivariate Student's t-distribution. multi_cauchy(mean, Sigma): Multivariate Cauchy distribution (Student-t df=1). gaussian_process(x, mean, magnitude, smoothing, noise): Gaussian process prior squared exponential kernel. lkj_corr(eta): LKJ prior correlation matrices. dirichlet(alpha): Dirichlet distribution simplexes. lower_tri_normal(mean, sd): Normal distribution elements lower-triangular matrix. centered_tri_multi_normal(sigma): Multivariate normal centered triangular matrices (used identification constraints). sufficient_multi_normal_fa(S_mat, N, y_bar, mu, psi, Lambda): Factor analysis likelihood using sufficient statistics (highly efficient large sample sizes). Response-time Distributions: diffusion(alpha, tau, beta, delta): Drift diffusion model likelihood response times binary choices. Use obs(RT, Choice) ~ diffusion(...). Vectorization: univariate distributions vectorized. y mu vectors, y ~ normal(mu, sigma) calculate sum log-densities elements efficiently.

what: Mathematical and Matrix Utility Functions for RTMB Models — math_functions
title: Mathematical and Matrix Utility Functions for RTMB Models — math_functions
text: page summarizes utility functions provided assist writing efficient numerically stable model code within `rtmb_code`. functions specifically designed compatible RTMB's Automatic Differentiation (AD).

title: Mathematical and Matrix Utility Functions for RTMB Models — math_functions
text: 1. Link Inverse Functions: logit(x): Computes logit transformation log(x/(1-x)). inv_logit(x): Computes inverse logit (logistic) transformation 1/(1+exp(-x)). 2. Numerical Stability Utilities: functions use specialized algorithms prevent overflow/underflow AD calculations. log_sum_exp(x): Safely computes log(sum(exp(x))) using log-sum-exp trick. log1p_exp(x): Computes log(1 + exp(x)) stably. log1m_exp(x): Computes log(1 - exp(x)) stably x < 0. log_softmax(x): Computes log softmax function. fabs(x): smooth version absolute value function abs(x) ensure differentiability zero. 3. Matrix Vector Transformations: Used convert unconstrained vectors structured matrices (e.g., factor analysis identification constraints). sum_to_zero(x): Transforms vector length K-1 vector length K sums zero. to_lower_tri(x, M, D): Fills matrix size M x D elements vector x lower-triangular fashion. to_centered_matrix(x, R, C): Creates R x C matrix column sums zero. to_centered_tri(x, R, C): Creates R x C matrix column-wise sum--zero constraints lower elements (useful identification factor analysis). rtmb_vector(value, length): Creates AD-compatible vector initialized value. rtmb_array(value, dim): Creates AD-compatible array initialized value. 4. Linear Algebra AD: log_det_chol(L): Calculates log-determinant covariance matrix Cholesky factor L. quad_form_chol(x, L): Computes quadratic form x^T Sigma^(-1) x using Cholesky factor L. distance(x, y): Computes Euclidean distance two vectors small epsilon stability.

what: Parameter Types and Constraints in RTMB Models — parameter_types
title: Parameter Types and Constraints in RTMB Models — parameter_types
text: declaring parameters parameters block using Dim(), can specify type automatically apply structural constraints. constraints ensure parameters remain within valid mathematical space estimation.

title: Parameter Types and Constraints in RTMB Models — parameter_types
text: Standard Types: "vector", "matrix", "array": Standard unconstrained containers.   Use lower upper element-wise bounds. Ordering Constraints: "ordered": vector $x_1 < x_2 < ... < x_K$. "positive_ordered": vector $0 < x_1 < x_2 < ... < x_K$. Constrained Vectors: "simplex": vector elements >= 0 sum(x) = 1. "centered": vector length K sum(x) = 0. (Estimated using K-1 degrees freedom). Correlation Covariance Matrices: "corr_matrix": symmetric, positive-definite correlation matrix (diagonal elements 1). "cov_matrix": symmetric, positive-definite covariance matrix. "CF_corr": Cholesky factor correlation matrix. "CF_cov": Cholesky factor covariance matrix (diagonal elements positive). Special Structural Matrices: "lower_tri": lower-triangular matrix. "positive_lower_tri": lower-triangular matrix positive diagonal elements. "centered_matrix": matrix column sums zero. "centered_tri": triangular matrix column-wise sum--zero constraints (often used identification factor analysis).

text: delta Item coordinate matrix (M items x D dimensions), fitted object `EAP()` method. theta Person coordinate matrix (N persons x D dimensions). `delta` fitted object `theta` `NULL`, extracted fit. item_alpha Optional item alpha vector used item radii. `delta` fitted object `item_alpha` `NULL`, `alpha` extracted available. phi Deprecated alias `item_alpha`. dims Integer vector length 2 specifying dimensions plot. radius Numeric plot radius. `NULL`, radius chosen coordinates. signs Numeric vector length 2. Use `-1` flip axis. item_labels Optional item labels. Defaults row names item numbers. show_radius Logical; whether draw radius circles based item alpha. show_density Logical; whether draw density contours `theta`. circle_scale Numeric multiplier item radii. alpha Circle transparency. contour_n Grid size passed `MASS::kde2d()`. distance Character; `"auto"`, `"squared"`, `"euclidean"`. Used transform item alpha values plotted radii. `"auto"` uses fit's stored distance available. point_estimate Character; point estimate used `delta` fit object. Passed `estimate()`. prefer_rotated Logical; `delta` fitted object, prefer rotated generated quantities (`delta_rot` `theta_rot`) available. show_phi Deprecated alias `show_radius`. main, xlab, ylab Plot title axis labels. ... Additional arguments passed `plot()`.

code: # \donttest{   data(debate, package = "BayesRTMB")   mdl <- rtmb_lm(sat ~ talk + perf, data = debate) #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...   fit <- mdl$sample(sampling = 200, warmup = 200, chains = 2) #> Starting sequential sampling (chains = 2)... #> chain 1 started... #> chain 1: iter 200/400 (50%) warmup #> chain 1: iter 400/400 (100%) sampling #> chain 1 done (100%) #> chain 2 started... #> chain 2: iter 200/400 (50%) warmup #> chain 2: iter 400/400 (100%) sampling #> chain 2 done (100%) #> sampling: 100%   r <- rhat_summary(fit)   r #> R-hat summary #>  n min median  q90  q95  max n_gt_1.01 n_gt_1.05 #>  5   1      1 1.03 1.03 1.03         2         0   hist(r)  # }

what: Define an RTMB Model with Stan-like Syntax — rtmb_code
title: Define an RTMB Model with Stan-like Syntax — rtmb_code
text: rtmb_code primary interface defining Bayesian Frequentist models within RTMB framework. allows organize model logic functional blocks utilizing Stan-like ~ operator specifying priors likelihoods.

title: Define an RTMB Model with Stan-like Syntax — rtmb_code
code: rtmb_code(...)

title: Define an RTMB Model with Stan-like Syntax — rtmb_code
text: ... Model definition blocks.

title: Define an RTMB Model with Stan-like Syntax — rtmb_code
text: list unevaluated code blocks.

title: Define an RTMB Model with Stan-like Syntax — rtmb_code
text: Key Blocks Logic: setup: Pre-compilation data processing. parameters: Declaration estimated parameters. See parameter_types available constraints. transform: Definition intermediate variables. Use math_functions stable AD calculations. model: Likelihood Priors. Refer distributions available sampling statements. generate: Post-hoc calculation predictions diagnostics. Automatic Differentiation (AD) Note: code within parameters, transform, model automatically differentiated RTMB. ensure numerical stability, use provided utility functions like log_sum_exp inv_logit instead raw algebraic implementations.

title: Define an RTMB Model with Stan-like Syntax — rtmb_code
code: # \donttest{ # Simulate data for a linear regression set.seed(123) N <- 100 x <- rnorm(N) y <- 2.0 + 1.5 * x + rnorm(N, mean = 0, sd = 1) data_list <- list(N = N, x = x, y = y)  # Define the model using rtmb_code code <- rtmb_code(   setup = {     # Center the predictor variable (executed once)     x_centered <- x - mean(x)   },   parameters = {     # Define parameters and their constraints     alpha = Dim(1)     beta  = Dim(1)     sigma = Dim(1, lower = 0)   },   transform = {     # Calculate the linear predictor     mu <- alpha + beta * x_centered   },   model = {     # Priors     alpha ~ normal(0, 10)     beta  ~ normal(0, 10)     sigma ~ exponential(1)      # Likelihood (Vectorized)     y ~ normal(mu, sigma)   },   generate = {     # Calculate generated quantities     y_pred <- mu      # Must return a named list     list(y_pred = y_pred)   } )  # Create the model object mod <- rtmb_model(data = data_list, code = code) #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...  # Fit the model using MAP estimation map_res <- mod$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>  map_res$summary(pars = c("alpha", "beta", "sigma")) #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 145.34 #> Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -149.89 #>  #> Point Estimates and 95% Wald CI: #> variable  Estimate  Std. Error  Lower 95%  Upper 95%  #> alpha      2.02788     0.09564    1.84044    2.21532  #> beta       1.44737     0.10530    1.24099    1.65375  #> sigma      0.95640     0.06715    0.83344    1.09749  #>   # The generated quantity 'y_pred' can also be summarized map_res$summary("y_pred", max_rows = 5) #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 145.34 #> Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -149.89 #>  #> Point Estimates and 95% Wald CI: #>  variable  Estimate  Std. Error  Lower 95%  Upper 95%  #> y_pred[1]   1.08581     0.11766    0.85521    1.31642  #> y_pred[2]   1.56387     0.10142    1.36510    1.76265  #> y_pred[3]   4.15305     0.18179    3.79674    4.50936  #> y_pred[4]   1.99908     0.09566    1.81159    2.18656  #> y_pred[5]   2.08415     0.09572    1.89654    2.27177  #>  # }

what: Fit a Correlation Model using RTMB — rtmb_corr
title: Fit a Correlation Model using RTMB — rtmb_corr
text: `rtmb_corr` fits correlation model estimate means, standard deviations, correlation structures. supports simple correlation, multilevel correlation, classical frequentist estimation.

title: Fit a Correlation Model using RTMB — rtmb_corr
code: rtmb_corr(   x = NULL,   data = NULL,   ID = NULL,   covariates = NULL,   method = c("pearson", "spearman", "reml"),   prior = prior_flat(),   y_range = NULL,   init = NULL,   fixed = NULL,   missing = c("listwise", "fiml", "pairwise"),   WAIC = FALSE )

title: Fit a Correlation Model using RTMB — rtmb_corr
text: x matrix, data frame, formula, expression (e.g., cbind(V1, V2)) response variables. data optional data frame containing variables. ID character string expression specifying group ID variable multilevel models. covariates Optional numeric matrix data frame covariates included joint MVN model. method Correlation method classic(): "pearson", "spearman", "reml". prior Prior configuration object: `prior_flat()`, `prior_normal()`, `prior_weak()`. Default `prior_flat()`. y_range Optional numeric vector matrix defining theoretical range (min, max) response variables. Required using prior_weak(). Can vector length 2 (applies variables) matrix/list length P. init Optional list initial values. fixed Optional named list fixed values specific parameters. missing Missing value handling strategy: "listwise" (default), "pairwise", "fiml" (Full Information Maximum Likelihood). WAIC Logical; TRUE, add pointwise `log_lik` generate block WAIC.

title: RTMB-based Factor Analysis Wrapper — rtmb_fa
text: Performs exploratory factor analysis (EFA) using RTMB. supports standard rotation methods (e.g., varimax, promax) well regularized factor analysis using Spike--Slab Prior (SSP) estimating sparse loading matrices.

title: RTMB-based Factor Analysis Wrapper — rtmb_fa
code: rtmb_fa(   data,   nfactors = 1,   rotate = NULL,   score = FALSE,   prior = prior_flat(),   y_range = NULL,   init = NULL,   fixed = NULL,   missing = c("listwise", "fiml"),   WAIC = FALSE )

title: RTMB-based Factor Analysis Wrapper — rtmb_fa
text: data Observation data frame matrix (N x J). nfactors Number factors (K). rotate String specifying rotation method (e.g., "varimax", "promax", "ssp"). NULL, rotation applied. Specifying "ssp" performs regularized factor analysis. score Logical; TRUE, factor scores calculated generate block (default FALSE). prior Prior configuration: `prior_flat()`, `prior_normal()`, `prior_weak()`. `prior_ssp()` accepted `rotate = "ssp"`. Hyperparameters can specified within functions (e.g., `prior_normal(mean_sd = 10, sd_rate = 10)`). Available parameters FA: `mean_sd`, `sd_rate`, `loadings_sd`, `ssp_ratio` (`rotate = "ssp"`). y_range numeric vector length 2 specifying theoretical min max values items. Used construct weakly informative priors `prior = prior_weak()`. init List initial values. fixed named list parameter values fix (optional). missing Missing value handling strategy: "listwise" (default) "fiml" (Full Information Maximum Likelihood). WAIC Logical; TRUE, add pointwise `log_lik` generate block WAIC.

title: RTMB-based Factor Analysis Wrapper — rtmb_fa
code: # Prepare a subset of the BigFive dataset for factor analysis   data(BigFive, package = "BayesRTMB")   fa_data <- BigFive[, 1:10]    # --- 1. Standard Exploratory Factor Analysis (1 Factor) ---   fit_fa1 <- rtmb_fa(data = fa_data, nfactors = 1) #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...    # Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation   map_fa1 <- fit_fa1$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>    map_fa1$summary() #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 2485.47 #> Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -2535.33 #>  #> Point Estimates and 95% Wald CI: #>        variable  Estimate  Std. Error  Lower 95%  Upper 95%  #> L[BF1,Factor1]    0.05250     0.08868   -0.12130    0.22630  #> L[BF2,Factor1]    0.79972     0.07145    0.65968    0.93976  #> L[BF3,Factor1]   -0.13478     0.08707   -0.30542    0.03587  #> L[BF4,Factor1]   -0.20465     0.08257   -0.36649   -0.04282  #> L[BF5,Factor1]   -0.14443     0.08455   -0.31015    0.02128  #> L[BF6,Factor1]   -0.00682     0.08660   -0.17655    0.16291  #> L[BF7,Factor1]    0.84338     0.07318    0.69994    0.98681  #> L[BF8,Factor1]   -0.24816     0.08298   -0.41080   -0.08551  #> L[BF9,Factor1]   -0.11264     0.08648   -0.28214    0.05686  #> L[BF10,Factor1]   0.21358     0.08794    0.04122    0.38593  #>       # --- 2. Factor Analysis with Factor Scores and Post-hoc Rotation (2 Factors) ---   # Extract 2 factors and calculate factor scores   fit_fa2 <- rtmb_fa(data = fa_data, nfactors = 2, score = TRUE) #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...    # Setting se_method = "sampling" enables standard errors and 95% CIs   # for transformed and generated quantities, such as factor scores and post-hoc rotations.   # (It uses multivariate normal sampling from the unconstrained parameter space)   map_fa2 <- fit_fa2$optimize(se_method = "sampling") #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>  #> Using simulation-based error propagation (1000 samples)...   map_fa2$summary() #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 2440.43 #> Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -2503.48 #>  #> Point Estimates and 95% Sampling-based CI: #>        variable  Estimate  Std. Error  Lower 95%  Upper 95%  #> L[BF1,Factor1]   -0.88470     0.18969   -0.99419   -0.29384  #> L[BF2,Factor1]   -0.10358     0.08347   -0.26604    0.06702  #> L[BF3,Factor1]   -0.11063     0.08073   -0.26601    0.04442  #> L[BF4,Factor1]   -0.07090     0.09204   -0.24860    0.12006  #> L[BF5,Factor1]    0.04105     0.08525   -0.12575    0.20137  #> L[BF6,Factor1]    0.68758     0.12657    0.38931    0.88475  #> L[BF7,Factor1]   -0.01923     0.08295   -0.18019    0.14095  #> L[BF8,Factor1]   -0.14559     0.09766   -0.34376    0.04672  #> L[BF9,Factor1]    0.08890     0.09521   -0.10913    0.27118  #> L[BF10,Factor1]  -0.11707     0.08321   -0.27523    0.04973  #>     # Post-hoc rotation using the fa_rotate() method   # You can rotate the unrotated loading matrix ("L") and factor scores after estimation.   map_fa2$fa_rotate(target = "L", scores = "score", rotate = "promax") #> Applying promax rotation to L (Saving to generate as _promax)... #> Generated quantities updated.    # The post-hoc rotated loadings are automatically stored with the method's   # suffix (e.g., "L_promax")   map_fa2$summary("L_promax") #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 2440.43 #> Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -2503.48 #>  #> Point Estimates and 95% Sampling-based CI: #>       variable  Estimate  Std. Error  Lower 95%  Upper 95%  #> L_promax[1,1]   -0.88412     0.19040   -0.99590   -0.29552  #> L_promax[2,1]   -0.07323     0.04808   -0.16583    0.02137  #> L_promax[3,1]   -0.11607     0.07986   -0.27315    0.04090  #> L_promax[4,1]   -0.07882     0.08795   -0.24846    0.09597  #> L_promax[5,1]    0.03564     0.08367   -0.12772    0.19089  #> L_promax[6,1]    0.68867     0.12762    0.39206    0.88820  #> L_promax[7,1]    0.01309     0.04747   -0.07218    0.09987  #> L_promax[8,1]   -0.15549     0.09734   -0.35668    0.02904  #> L_promax[9,1]    0.08481     0.09385   -0.10586    0.26402  #> L_promax[10,1]  -0.10916     0.07976   -0.26512    0.04661  #>       # --- 3. Regularized Factor Analysis using Spike-and-Slab Prior (SSP) ---   # Specifying 'rotate = "ssp"' enables sparse loading matrix estimation   fit_ssp <- rtmb_fa(data = fa_data, nfactors = 2, rotate = "ssp") #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...    map_ssp <- fit_ssp$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>  #> Warning: No optimization run reached an acceptable optimizer status; BEST is selected by the lowest objective among not converged runs ( convergence code = 1; message = function evaluation limit reached without convergence (9)). text: RTMB-based GLM wrapper function (random effects)

title: RTMB-based GLM wrapper function (no random effects) — rtmb_glm
code: rtmb_glm(   formula,   data,   family = "gaussian",   prior = prior_flat(),   y_range = NULL,   init = NULL,   fixed = NULL,   gmc = NULL,   centering = NULL,   view = NULL,   factors = NULL,   contrasts = "treatment",   missing = c("listwise", "fiml"),   WAIC = FALSE,   ... )

title: RTMB-based GLM wrapper function (no random effects) — rtmb_glm
text: formula Formula data Data frame family Character string distribution family (e.g., "gaussian", "binomial", "poisson") prior object class `"rtmb_prior"`. Use `prior_flat()` prior, `prior_normal()` default normal/exponential priors, `prior_jzs()` continuous-family JZS priors, `prior_weak()`, `prior_rhs()`, `prior_ssp()` weakly informative regularized Bayesian inference. Default `prior_flat()`. y_range Theoretical minimum maximum values response variable vector c(min, max). Specifying automatically enables weakly informative priors. init List initial values fixed Optional named list fixed values specific parameters. gmc Character vector variable names GMC centering Alias `gmc`. view Optional character vector parameter names show first summary. factors Character vector variable names treated factors. contrasts Character string specifying contrast type ("treatment" "sum"). missing Missing value handling strategy: "listwise". WAIC Logical; TRUE, add pointwise `log_lik` generate block WAIC. ... Additional arguments passed rtmb_glmer().

title: RTMB-based GLM wrapper function (no random effects) — rtmb_glm
code: # --- 1. Linear Regression (rtmb_lm) ---   # Fit a linear regression model using the debate dataset   data(debate, package = "BayesRTMB")   fit_lm <- rtmb_lm(sat ~ talk + perf, data = debate) #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...   map_lm <- fit_lm$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>    map_lm$summary() #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 395.58 #> Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -404.61 #>  #> Point Estimates and 95% Wald CI: #>  variable  Estimate  Std. Error  Lower 95%  Upper 95%  #> Intercept   1.83366     0.21105    1.42000    2.24732  #> b[talk]     0.28694     0.05291    0.18323    0.39064  #> b[perf]     0.15632     0.02987    0.09777    0.21486  #> sigma       0.90453     0.03693    0.83497    0.97988  #>     # --- 2. title: RTMB-based GLMM wrapper function — rtmb_glmer
text: RTMB-based GLMM wrapper function

title: RTMB-based GLMM wrapper function — rtmb_glmer
code: rtmb_glmer(   formula,   data,   family = "gaussian",   laplace = FALSE,   prior = prior_flat(),   y_range = NULL,   init = NULL,   fixed = NULL,   gmc = NULL,   centering = NULL,   cwc = NULL,   view = NULL,   within = NULL,   factors = NULL,   contrasts = "treatment",   sigma_by = NULL,   resid_corr = NULL,   resid_time = NULL,   resid_group = NULL,   generate = NULL,   missing = c("listwise", "fiml"),   WAIC = FALSE,   .force_sum = FALSE )

title: RTMB-based GLMM wrapper function — rtmb_glmer
text: formula lme4-style formula (e.g., Y ~ X + (1 | GID)) data Data frame family Character string distribution family (e.g., "gaussian", "binomial", "poisson", "ordered", "sequential") laplace Logical; whether marginalize random effects using Laplace approximation prior object class "rtmb_prior" specifying prior distribution. Use `prior_flat()`, `prior_normal()`, `prior_weak()`, `prior_rhs()`, `prior_ssp()`. `prior_jzs()` available continuous families. Default `prior_flat()`. y_range Theoretical minimum maximum values response variable vector c(min, max). Required using weakly informative regularized priors continuous models. init List initial values (generated automatically based glm omitted) fixed Optional named list fixed values specific parameters. gmc Character vector variable names Grand Mean Centering (GMC). "", numeric variables centered. centering Alias `gmc`. cwc List Centering Within Cluster (CWC). contain cluster (group variable) pars (variable names center). can also use cwc = list(ID, "x") cwc = list(ID, ""); "" centers numeric fixed-effect variables within cluster. view Character vector parameter names prioritize summary. within Optional list wide--long conversion. factors Character vector variable names treated factors. contrasts Character string specifying contrast type ("treatment" "sum"). sigma_by Character vector specifying variables group residual variance (heteroscedasticity). resid_corr Residual correlation structure: "ar1" (Autoregressive), "cs" (Compound Symmetry), "toep" (Toeplitz), "un" (Unstructured). resid_time Variable name time points residual correlation. resid_group Variable name grouping residual correlation. generate Optional expression generated quantities. missing Missing value handling strategy: "listwise". WAIC Logical; TRUE, add pointwise `log_lik` generate block WAIC. .force_sum Logical; internal use .

what: RTMB-based IRT (Item Response Theory) Wrapper — rtmb_irt
title: RTMB-based IRT (Item Response Theory) Wrapper — rtmb_irt
text: Performs Item Response Theory modeling (1PL, 2PL, 3PL, Graded Response Model) using RTMB. Missing values (NA) data automatically removed internally efficient computation.

title: RTMB-based IRT (Item Response Theory) Wrapper — rtmb_irt
code: rtmb_irt(   data,   model = c("2PL", "1PL", "3PL"),   type = c("binary", "ordered"),   prior = prior_flat(),   init = NULL,   fixed = NULL,   view = NULL,   missing = c("listwise", "fiml"),   WAIC = FALSE )

title: RTMB-based IRT (Item Response Theory) Wrapper — rtmb_irt
text: data data frame matrix item responses (N persons x J items). model Character string model type: "1PL", "2PL", "3PL". type Character string data type: "binary" "ordered". prior Prior configuration: `prior_flat()`, `prior_normal()`, `prior_weak()`. Hyperparameters can specified within functions (e.g., `prior_normal(b_sd = 5)`). Available parameters IRT: `a_rate` (discrimination), `b_sd` (difficulty), `c_alpha`/`c_beta` (guessing). init List initial values. fixed named list parameter values fix (optional). view Character vector parameter names prioritize summary. missing Missing value handling strategy: "listwise" (default) "fiml" (Full Information Maximum Likelihood). WAIC Logical; TRUE, add pointwise `log_lik` generate block WAIC.

title: RTMB-based IRT (Item Response Theory) Wrapper — rtmb_irt
code: # --- 1. Binary Data (e.g., correct/incorrect answers) ---   # Simulate binary response data for 100 persons and 5 items   set.seed(123)   bin_data <- matrix(rbinom(500, size = 1, prob = 0.6), nrow = 100, ncol = 5)   colnames(bin_data) <- paste0("Item", 1:5)    # Introduce some missing values (NA) to demonstrate automatic handling   #bin_data[sample(1:500, 10)] <- NA    # Fit a 2-Parameter Logistic (2PL) model   fit_2pl <- rtmb_irt(data = bin_data, model = "2PL", type = "binary") #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...    # Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation   map_2pl <- fit_2pl$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>  #> Warning: No optimization run reached an acceptable optimizer status; BEST is selected by the lowest objective among not converged runs ( convergence code = 1; message = function evaluation limit reached without convergence (9)). Estimates may be unreliable; consider increasing num_estimate, changing initial values, or adjusting optimizer control settings.   map_2pl$summary() #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 334.06 #> Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -326.51 #> Note: Random effects are stored in $random_effects (use ranef = TRUE to show them) #>  #> Point Estimates and 95% Wald CI: #> variable     Estimate   Std. Error     Lower 95%       Upper 95%  #> a[Item1]      0.00017      0.00222       0.00000  14803403.02410  #> a[Item2]      0.00024      0.00268       0.00000    524214.81653  #> a[Item3]      0.33853      0.63664       0.00849        13.50088  #> a[Item4]      0.52727      0.55116       0.06796         4.09068  #> a[Item5]      0.33846      0.63717       0.00845        13.55001  #> b[Item1]  -2340.60797  30040.17736  -61218.27250     56537.05656  #> b[Item2]  -2001.47440  21926.82445  -44977.26074     40974.31193  #> b[Item3]     -1.35900      2.49182      -6.24287         3.52488  #> b[Item4]     -0.90809      0.92095      -2.71311         0.89693  #> b[Item5]     -1.35941      2.49533      -6.25018         3.53135  #>     # --- 2. Ordered Data (e.g., Likert scale) ---   # Simulate ordered response data (categories 1 to 5) with a true latent structure   set.seed(123)   N <- 100   J <- 5   K <- 4    # True parameters   theta <- rnorm(N, 0, 1)          # Person abilities (latent traits)   a <- runif(J, 0.8, 2.0)          # Item discriminations    # Item-specific thresholds (must be strictly increasing)   b <- matrix(NA, nrow = J, ncol = K - 1)   for (j in 1:J) {     b[j, ] <- sort(c(-1.5, 0, 1.5) + rnorm(3, 0, 0.2))   }    ord_data <- matrix(NA, nrow = N, ncol = J)   colnames(ord_data) <- paste0("Item", 1:J)    # Generate responses based on the Graded Response Model   for (i in 1:N) {     for (j in 1:J) {       # Latent continuous response (eta + standard logistic noise)       eta <- a[j] * theta[i]       y_star <- eta + rlogis(1)        # Apply thresholds to determine the observed category (1 to 4)       category <- 1       for (k in 1:(K - 1)) {         if (y_star > b[j, k]) category <- k + 1       }       ord_data[i, j] <- category     }   }    # Fit a Graded Response Model (2PL for ordered data)   fit_ord <- rtmb_irt(data = ord_data, model = "2PL", type = "ordered") #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...   fit_ord$print_code() #> === RTMB Model Code === #>  #> rtmb_code( #>   setup = { #>     obs_data <- which(!is.na(Y), arr.ind = TRUE) #>     person_idx <- as.integer(obs_data[, "row"]) #>     item_idx <- as.integer(obs_data[, "col"]) #>     Y_obs <- Y[obs_data] #>     N_persons <- nrow(Y) #>     N_items <- ncol(Y) #>     N_obs <- length(Y_obs) #>     if (min(Y_obs) == 0)  #>       Y_obs <- Y_obs + 1 #>     K_cat <- max(Y_obs) #>   },  #>   parameters = { #>     theta <- Dim(N_persons, random = TRUE) #>     b <- Dim(c(N_items, K_cat - 1), type = "ordered") #>     a <- Dim(N_items, lower = 0) #>   },  #>   model = { #>     # Likelihood #>     for (i in 1:N_obs) { #>       p <- person_idx[i] #>       j <- item_idx[i] #>       y <- Y_obs[i] #>       eta <- a[j] * theta[p] #>       y ~ ordered_logistic(eta, b[j, ]) #>     } #>     # Priors #>     theta ~ normal(0, 1) #>   } #> )    map_ord <- fit_ord$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>    map_ord$summary() #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 652.00 #> Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -661.02 #> Note: Random effects are stored in $random_effects (use ranef = TRUE to show them) #>  #> Point Estimates and 95% Wald CI: #>            variable  Estimate  Std. title: RTMB-based Linear Regression wrapper function — rtmb_lm
code: rtmb_lm(   formula,   data,   prior = prior_flat(),   y_range = NULL,   init = NULL,   fixed = NULL,   gmc = NULL,   centering = NULL,   view = NULL,   factors = NULL,   contrasts = "treatment",   missing = c("listwise", "fiml"),   WAIC = FALSE,   ... )

title: RTMB-based Linear Regression wrapper function — rtmb_lm
text: formula Formula (e.g., Y ~ X1 + X2) data Data frame prior object class `"rtmb_prior"`. Use `prior_flat()` prior, `prior_normal()` default normal/exponential priors, `prior_jzs()` JZS regression priors, `prior_weak()`, `prior_rhs()`, `prior_ssp()` weakly informative regularized Bayesian inference. Default `prior_flat()`. y_range Theoretical minimum maximum values response variable vector c(min, max). Specifying automatically enables weakly informative priors. init List initial values. fixed Optional named list fixed values specific parameters. gmc Character vector variable names GMC centering Alias `gmc`. view Optional character vector parameter names show first summary. factors Character vector variable names treated factors. contrasts Character string specifying contrast type ("treatment" "sum"). missing Missing value handling strategy: "listwise". WAIC Logical; TRUE, add pointwise `log_lik` generate block WAIC. ... Additional arguments passed rtmb_glmer().

title: RTMB-based Linear Regression wrapper function — rtmb_lm

Linear Regression (rtmb_lm) ---   # Fit a linear regression model using the debate dataset   data(debate, package = "BayesRTMB")   fit_lm <- rtmb_lm(sat ~ talk + perf, data = debate) #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...   map_lm <- fit_lm$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>    map_lm$summary() #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 395.58 #> Approx. code: rtmb_lmer(   formula,   data,   laplace = TRUE,   prior = prior_flat(),   y_range = NULL,   init = NULL,   fixed = NULL,   gmc = NULL,   centering = NULL,   cwc = NULL,   view = NULL,   sigma_by = NULL,   factors = NULL,   contrasts = "treatment",   resid_corr = NULL,   resid_time = NULL,   resid_group = NULL,   within = NULL,   missing = c("listwise", "fiml"),   WAIC = FALSE,   ... )

title: RTMB-based Linear Mixed Model (LMM) wrapper function — rtmb_lmer
text: formula Formula data Data frame laplace Logical; whether marginalize random effects using Laplace approximation prior object class `"rtmb_prior"`. Use `prior_flat()` prior, `prior_normal()` default normal/exponential priors, `prior_jzs()` JZS regression priors, `prior_weak()`, `prior_rhs()`, `prior_ssp()` weakly informative regularized Bayesian inference. Default `prior_flat()`. y_range Theoretical minimum maximum values response variable init Initial values fixed Optional named list fixed values specific parameters. gmc Character vector variable names GMC centering Alias `gmc`. cwc List CWC view Character vector parameter names prioritize summary. sigma_by Character vector specifying variables group residual variance (heteroscedasticity). factors Character vector variable names treated factors. contrasts Character string specifying contrast type ("treatment" "sum"). resid_corr Residual correlation structure (e.g., "ar1", "cs", "un", "toep"). resid_time Variable name time points residual correlation. resid_group Variable name grouping residual correlation. within Optional list wide--long conversion. repeated measures data wide format, specify factor names levels, e.g., list(Time = 4) list(= 2, B = 3). total number levels must match number columns cbind() LHS. omitted LHS cbind(), within-factor name inferred RHS variables present data. missing Missing value handling strategy: "listwise". WAIC Logical; TRUE, add pointwise `log_lik` generate block WAIC. ... Additional arguments passed rtmb_glmer().

title: RTMB-based Log-linear analysis (Poisson regression) — rtmb_loglinear

title: RTMB-based Log-linear analysis (Poisson regression) — rtmb_loglinear

title: RTMB-based Log-linear analysis (Poisson regression) — rtmb_loglinear
text: formula formula (e.g., `~ + B + :B`) contingency table. data data frame (required `formula` used). prior object class "rtmb_prior" specifying prior distribution. y_range Optional theoretical minimum maximum values count response. supplied default flat prior, `prior_weak()` used. fixed Optional named list fixed values specific parameters. WAIC Logical; TRUE, add pointwise `log_lik` generate block WAIC. ... Additional arguments passed `rtmb_glm()`.

title: RTMB-based Multidimensional Unfolding Wrapper — rtmb_mdu
text: Fits multidimensional unfolding model preference/rating data. Rows persons observations columns stimuli/items. model represents row scores (`theta`) item locations (`delta`) shared D-dimensional space.

title: RTMB-based Multidimensional Unfolding Wrapper — rtmb_mdu
code: rtmb_mdu(   data,   ndim = 2,   distance = c("euclidean", "squared"),   alpha = c("random", "fix"),   lambda = c("fix", "random"),   method = c("rating", "Best", "Best-Worst", "MDS"),   sets = NULL,   prior = prior_flat(),   y_range = NULL,   init = NULL,   fixed = NULL,   view = NULL,   distance_eps = 1e-04,   missing = c("listwise", "fiml"),   WAIC = FALSE )

title: RTMB-based Multidimensional Unfolding Wrapper — rtmb_mdu
text: data Numeric matrix data frame (N rows x M items) `method = "rating"`. choice methods, list containing `Best` , `method = "Best-Worst"`, `Worst`. `method = "Best-Worst"`, single matrix/data frame treated pre-coded `Y_dif` pair indices. ndim Number unfolding dimensions. distance Character; `"euclidean"` uses Euclidean distance (default), `"squared"` uses squared Euclidean distance. alpha Character; `"random"` estimates item-specific alpha values random effects (default), `"fix"` estimates single common alpha. lambda Character; choice methods, `"fix"` estimates common inverse-temperature parameter (default), `"random"` estimates person-specific inverse-temperature parameters log-normal hierarchy. method Character; `"rating"` continuous ratings, `"Best"` best-choice tasks, `"Best-Worst"` best-worst choice tasks, `"MDS"` fitting multidimensional scaling model distance matrix. sets Matrix data frame presented item sets (P tasks x C items) choice methods. prior Prior configuration: `prior_flat()`, `prior_normal()`, `prior_weak()`. `prior_flat()` creates maximum-likelihood model suitable `classic()`. latent coordinates `delta` `theta` always treated random effects normal scale priors, similarly IRT ability parameters. y_range Optional response range `method = "rating"`. supplied default flat prior, `prior_weak()` used. choice methods (`"Best"` `"Best-Worst"`), `prior_weak()` behaves like `prior_normal()` observed rating scale. init Optional named list initial values. fixed Optional named list parameter values fix. view Character vector parameter names prioritize summaries. distance_eps Small positive constant added distance. missing Missing value handling strategy: "listwise" (default) "fiml" (Full Information Maximum Likelihood). WAIC Logical; TRUE, add pointwise `log_lik` generate block WAIC.

title: RTMB-based Multidimensional Unfolding Wrapper — rtmb_mdu
text: `RTMB_Model` object.

title: RTMB-based Multidimensional Unfolding Wrapper — rtmb_mdu
code: # Simulate rating data for Multidimensional Unfolding (MDU)   set.seed(123)   N <- 50  # Number of persons   M <- 10  # Number of items   D <- 2   # Number of dimensions      # True person and item coordinates in a 2D space   theta <- matrix(rnorm(N * D), N, D)   delta <- matrix(rnorm(M * D), M, D)      # Generate distance-like ratings (smaller means more preferred)   Y <- matrix(NA, N, M)   for(i in 1:N) {     for(j in 1:M) {       Y[i, j] <- sum((theta[i,] - delta[j,])^2) + rnorm(1, 0, 0.5)     }   }    # Fit a 2-dimensional MDU model   fit_mdu <- rtmb_mdu(Y, ndim = 2, distance = "squared", method = "rating") #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...      # MAP estimation   map_mdu <- fit_mdu$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>    map_mdu$summary() #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 977.95 #> Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -993.42 #> Note: Random effects are stored in $random_effects (use ranef = TRUE to show them) #>  #> Point Estimates and 95% Wald CI: #>  variable  Estimate  Std. Error  Lower 95%  Upper 95%  #> alpha[1]    3.24149     0.48954    2.28202    4.20097  #> alpha[2]    3.07680     0.48789    2.12055    4.03304  #> alpha[3]    4.58333     0.74269    3.12768    6.03898  #> alpha[4]    2.89562     0.47457    1.96548    3.82575  #> alpha[5]    3.94807     0.61796    2.73689    5.15926  #> alpha[6]    2.90950     0.47861    1.97145    3.84756  #> alpha[7]    3.31641     0.49573    2.34480    4.28801  #> alpha[8]    4.16539     0.64349    2.90418    5.42660  #> alpha[9]    3.34095     0.50248    2.35611    4.32579  #> alpha[10]   3.86744     0.59089    2.70931    5.02557  #>       # Note: MDU models have many parameters, so MCMC sampling might take time.   # \donttest{   # mcmc_mdu <- fit_mdu$sample(sampling = 500, warmup = 500, chains = 2)   # }

title: RTMB-based Mediation Analysis Wrapper — rtmb_mediation
text: function identifies mediation paths looking variables responses one equation predictors another. Indirect effects calculated product coefficients along paths (\(* b\)). Uncertainty Estimation: using `$optimize(ci_method = "sampling")`, function provides asymmetric confidence intervals indirect effects based distribution products, accurate standard Sobel test (Delta Method).

title: RTMB-based Mediation Analysis Wrapper — rtmb_mediation
code: # Simulate mediation data   set.seed(123)   N <- 100   X <- rnorm(N)   # M is influenced by X   M <- 0.5 * X + rnorm(N, 0, 0.5)   # Y is influenced by both X and M   Y <- 0.3 * X + 0.8 * M + rnorm(N, 0, 0.5)   dat <- data.frame(X, M, Y)    # Fit a mediation model   # The formula list specifies the two regression equations   fit_med <- rtmb_mediation(list(M ~ X, Y ~ X + M), data = dat) #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...      # Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation   map_med <- fit_med$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>    # The summary automatically calculates indirect, direct, and total effects   map_med$summary() #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 134.66 #> Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -149.35 #>  #> Point Estimates and 95% Wald CI: #>      variable  Estimate  Std. Error  Lower 95%  Upper 95%  #> b1[Intercept]  -0.05140     0.04829   -0.14604    0.04324  #> b1[X]           0.47376     0.05290    0.37008    0.57745  #> b2[Intercept]   0.06753     0.04734   -0.02526    0.16032  #> b2[X]           0.22151     0.06924    0.08580    0.35721  #> b2[M]           0.82381     0.09750    0.63272    1.01490  #> IE_X_M_Y        0.39029     0.06351    0.26582    0.51476  #> DE_X_Y          0.22151     0.06924    0.08580    0.35721  #> TE_X_M_Y        0.61180     0.06753    0.47945    0.74415  #> sigma1          0.48048     0.03398    0.41830    0.55191  #> sigma2          0.46846     0.03313    0.40783    0.53810  #>

title: Create an RTMB_Model Object — rtmb_model
text: `rtmb_model` function acts core constructor compiling combining user-defined data model code (defined via `rtmb_code`). generates `RTMB_Model` (R6 class) instance, serves foundation performing Bayesian inference, including Maximum Posteriori (MAP) estimation, Variational Inference (ADVI), Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling.

title: Create an RTMB_Model Object — rtmb_model
code: rtmb_model(   data,   code,   par_names = list(),   init = NULL,   view = NULL,   fixed = NULL,   silent = FALSE,   gr_test = FALSE )

title: Create an RTMB_Model Object — rtmb_model
text: data named list containing observation data constants (e.g., sample size, matrices) used model. code model definition block generated rtmb_code(...) (must include parameters model blocks). par_names named list character vectors corresponding dimensions specific parameters (optional). init list numeric vector initial values parameters (optional). specified, initialized randomly. view Character vector parameter names displayed preferentially top outputting results like summary() (optional). fixed named list parameter values fix (optional). Useful scoring plug-estimation parameters (e.g., item parameters) fixed known values. silent Logical; TRUE, suppresses diagnostic messages model creation. Default FALSE. gr_test Logical; TRUE, evaluate gradient AD tape built. default, model creation checks MakeADFun() can build AD tape skips extra gradient evaluation speed.

title: Create an RTMB_Model Object — rtmb_model
text: RTMB_Model class instance compiled pre-tested automatic differentiation function. RTMB_Model class instance compiled pre-tested automatic differentiation function.

title: Create an RTMB_Model Object — rtmb_model
text: Model Compilation Pre-checking: function called, evaluates provided data model blocks. performs "sandbox execution" (pre-check) using dummy initial values dynamically detect common structural errors, undefined variables, --bounds indices, incompatible matrix operations, proceeding computationally expensive Automatic Differentiation (MakeADFun) phase. Cryptic backend errors caught translated user-friendly hints. Writing AD-Compatible Code (Important): ensure model differentiable, must follow specific syntax rules writing code within rtmb_code. Avoid discrete branching (, ifelse) based parameters use numerically stable functions. See rtmb_syntax detailed guide Automatic Differentiation requirements. Initial Values (init): Initial parameter values can specified flat numeric vector named list. partial list provided, unspecified parameters automatically initialized random values drawn uniform distribution unconstrained scale. init = NULL, parameters initialized randomly. Parameter Labeling (par_names): default, vector matrix parameters displayed numeric indices (e.g., mu[1]). can pass named list character vectors par_names assign meaningful labels specific dimensions (e.g., mu[Control]), vastly improving readability summary outputs trace plots. Available Inference Methods Returned Object: returned RTMB_Model instance provides following core methods: $optimize(...): Performs Maximum Posteriori (MAP) Maximum Likelihood estimation. Returns MAP_Fit object. $sample(...): Draws posterior samples using NUTS (-U-Turn Sampler) algorithm. Returns MCMC_Fit object. $variational(...): Performs Mean-field Full-rank Automatic Differentiation Variational Inference (ADVI). Returns VB_Fit object.

title: Create an RTMB_Model Object — rtmb_model
code: # \donttest{ # Simulate data for 3 groups set.seed(123) N <- 60 group_idx <- sample(1:3, N, replace = TRUE) group_names <- c("Control", "Treatment_A", "Treatment_B")  # True group means: Control = 0, Treatment_A = 2, Treatment_B = -1 true_means <- c(0, 2, -1) y <- true_means[group_idx] + rnorm(N, mean = 0, sd = 0.5)  data_list <- list(N = N, K = 3, group_idx = group_idx, y = y)  # Define the model using rtmb_code model_code <- rtmb_code(   parameters = {     mu    = Dim(K)             # Vector of length K (group means)     sigma = Dim(1, lower = 0)  # Scalar (residual standard deviation)   },   model = {     # Priors     for (k in 1:K) mu[k] ~ normal(0, 10)     sigma ~ exponential(1)      # Likelihood     for (i in 1:N) {       y[i] ~ normal(mu[group_idx[i]], sigma)     }   } )  # --- 1. Basic Model Creation --- # Create the RTMB_Model object mod_basic <- rtmb_model(   data = data_list,   code = model_code ) #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...  # Perform Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation map_basic <- mod_basic$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>  # The summary displays default parameter names: mu[1], mu[2], mu[3] map_basic$summary() #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 46.99 #> Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -53.43 #>  #> Point Estimates and 95% Wald CI: #> variable  Estimate  Std. Error  Lower 95%  Upper 95%  #> mu[1]     -0.12319     0.09499   -0.30936    0.06298  #> mu[2]      2.15920     0.10806    1.94742    2.37099  #> mu[3]     -1.11887     0.09722   -1.30942   -0.92832  #> sigma      0.44555     0.04045    0.37292    0.53232  #>   # --- 2. Optional: Adding Custom Parameter Names and initial values --- # You can optionally use 'par_names' to assign meaningful labels # to vector or matrix elements for easier interpretation. mod_named <- rtmb_model(   data = data_list,   code = model_code,   init = list(mu = rep(0, 3), sigma = 1),   par_names = list(mu = group_names) ) #> Pre-checking model code... #> Checking RTMB setup...  map_named <- mod_named$optimize() #> Starting RTMB optimization... #>  # The summary now displays: mu[Control], mu[Treatment_A], mu[Treatment_B] map_named$summary() #>  #> Call: #> MAP Estimation via RTMB #>  #> Negative Log-Posterior: 46.99 #> Approx. Log Marginal Likelihood (Laplace): -53.43 #>  #> Point Estimates and 95% Wald CI: #>        variable  Estimate  Std. Error  Lower 95%  Upper 95%  #> mu[Control]      -0.12319     0.09499   -0.30936    0.06298  #> mu[Treatment_A]   2.15920     0.10806    1.94742    2.37099  #> mu[Treatment_B]  -1.11887     0.09722   -1.30942   -0.92832  #> sigma             0.44555     0.04045    0.37292    0.53232  #>  # }

what: Guidelines for Writing RTMB-Compatible Code — rtmb_syntax
title: Guidelines for Writing RTMB-Compatible Code — rtmb_syntax
text: Models defined rtmb_code rely Automatic Differentiation (AD) via RTMB package. ensure model differentiable numerically stable, specific coding practices must followed.

title: Guidelines for Writing RTMB-Compatible Code — rtmb_syntax
text: 1. Automatic Differentiation advector: Parameters intermediate calculations involving treated advector objects. RTMB "records" mathematical operations compute derivatives automatically. function strips attributes differentiable, gradient calculation fail. 2. Avoid Discrete Branching: Standard R conditional statements like (x > 0) ifelse() based parameter values provide derivatives. Problem: create "jumps" likelihood surface. Solution: Use smooth approximations. example, use fabs   instead abs, log_mix mixture logic. 3. Numerical Stability: Computations log-space preferred prevent overflow underflow. Use following stable utilities instead raw algebraic expressions: log_sum_exp: summing probabilities log-space. log1p_exp: log(1 + exp(x)). inv_logit: mapping real numbers probabilities. 4. Vectorization vs. Loops: Vectorization: Highly recommended performance. Standard R   vectorized arithmetic (+, -, *, /, log, exp)   works seamlessly AD. Loops: Standard loops safe long operations   inside differentiable. Avoid apply: Functions like apply, sapply,   lapply may sometimes strip AD attributes replaced   vectorized operations explicit loops. 5. Matrix Operations: Use specialized functions matrix algebra maintain efficiency: quad_form_chol: efficient quadratic forms. log_det_chol: log-determinants via Cholesky factors.
